risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Of these households, 4,043 were determined to be eligible and randomized to one of the three trial arms" and "A community-randomized controlled trial of UCTs and CCTs was conducted..." Comment: The paper states that eligible households were randomized and describes the study as community-randomized, but it does not report how the allocation sequence was generated (e.g. computer-generated random numbers, random number table, blocks, stratification, drawing lots). Because randomization is mentioned without sufficient detail or context clues about the sequence generation method, this domain is judged Unclear risk rather than High risk.

Summary: The paper states that eligible households were "randomized to one of the three trial arms," in a "community-randomized controlled trial," but provides no explicit description of how the upcoming assignments were concealed from the person enrolling households/participants. There is no mention of central allocation, sequentially numbered opaque sealed envelopes, pharmacy-controlled allocation, or another clear concealment mechanism. Comment: Randomization is reported, but allocation concealment is not described; therefore this domain is judged Unclear risk by default.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Households in the UCT and CCT arms received US$18 plus $4 per child in the household up to a maximum of 3 children. Heads of households received payments from designated locations every 2 months. Households in the CCT arm also were monitored for compliance with several conditions..." Comment: The nature of the intervention means participants and personnel delivering/monitoring the intervention would necessarily know assignment (cash transfer vs control; UCT vs CCT, with compliance monitoring in the CCT arm). Outcomes such as school attendance, birth registration, and child chronic illness/disability are plausibly influenced by knowledge of allocation and participant/implementer behavior, so lack of blinding could affect outcomes.

Summary: Outcomes were assessed largely by household/participant report in census/follow-up surveys rather than by a blinded independent assessor. The paper describes assignment to UCT, CCT, and control households and cash was delivered openly to heads of household, so participants would know allocation. Reported outcomes include subjective or judgement-dependent measures such as child chronic illness (defined as being "very sick for at least 3 months during the past 12 months") and disability ("an impairment that seriously affects ability to perform routine work, school and/or other daily functions"), which are vulnerable to biased measurement if reported by unblinded participants/caregivers. Although birth registration and school attendance are more objective, the study also includes these subjective outcomes; under the worst-outcome rule, one unblinded subjective outcome makes this domain High risk. Comment: Outcome assessor category is patient/caregiver self-report.

Quote: "Of these households, 4,043 were determined to be eligible and randomized to one of the three trial arms, and 3,818 (94 %) of these were followed up 1.5 years later." "For the current study, the sample was narrowed to 2,078 households who were caring for school-aged children ages 6–17 (N = 8,797 children). Of these 8,797 children, 31 were deceased, 396 were lost or were new to the study, and 514 left the household and were not follow-up with at the second wave of data collection, resulting in a sample of 7,857 children. From this sample, 7 % were missing school attendance data, 3 % were missing birth registration status, 8 % were missing chronic illness data, and 8 % were missing chronic disability data... The final sample included 5,331 school-aged children within 1,697 households." "The final sample included a higher percentage of participants in the UCT (p < .001) group and fewer participants in the control group (p < .001), a pattern which suggests greater attrition in control vs. treatment groups over time." Comment: For the analysed child-level outcomes, the paper reports substantial post-randomization loss from 8,797 eligible children to a final analysed sample of 5,331 children by 1.5 years (about 39% not in the final analysis), which exceeds the ~30% threshold. The paper also explicitly reports imbalance in attrition, with greater attrition in the control group than treatment groups. Reasons include children lost, leaving households, and outcome-level missingness. No robust missing-data analysis is reported to demonstrate that results were insensitive to this missingness. Therefore incomplete outcome data could plausibly bias the analysed outcomes.
